healthy_maize.description: Leaf looks green and clean with no clear disease marks.
stages: Healthy: Normal growth and healthy leaf appearance
healthy_maize.cause: Good field hygiene, balanced feeding, favorable weather, and proper crop care
healthy_maize.favorable_conditions: Healthy crop management and low disease pressure
management: Continue normal field monitoring every week
Keep balanced fertilizer and regular watering schedule
prevention: Use clean seed and resistant varieties
Maintain spacing for airflow and reduce leaf wetness

common_rust.description: Leaf has orange-brown rust pustules, often scattered in rows.
stages: Early: Small orange-brown pustules appear on lower leaves
Severe: Extensive pustules and fast spread across the plant
common_rust.cause: Rust fungus favored by humidity and moderate temperatures
common_rust.favorable_conditions: Warm, humid conditions with wind-borne spores
management: Start fungicide spray early
Monitor nearby plants for fast spread
Remove badly infected leaves if practical
prevention: Keep field clean and reduce volunteer maize plants

gray_leaf_spot.description: Leaf has gray rectangular spots that may join and spread quickly.
stages: Early: Small rectangular gray lesions appear on lower leaves
Moderate: Lesions enlarge and spread to mid-leaves
Severe: Large necrotic patches and defoliation begin
gray_leaf_spot.cause: Fungal infection in prolonged moisture conditions
gray_leaf_spot.favorable_conditions: Warm, humid, wet conditions with dense canopy
management: Apply fungicide and follow label timing
Reduce field humidity by improving spacing
Remove heavily damaged leaves where possible
prevention: Rotate crops and manage infected residues
Use certified seed and resistant hybrids

leaf_blight.description: Leaf shows long brown lesions that can expand along the blade.
stages: Early: Water-soaked, elongated lesions with yellow halo
Moderate: Lesions enlarge and tissue begins to die
Severe: Extensive dead areas and leaf shredding
leaf_blight.cause: Fungal infection in cool, wet conditions
leaf_blight.favorable_conditions: Cool, wet weather with poor airflow
management: Remove heavily affected leaves where possible
Apply recommended fungicide quickly
Improve spacing and avoid overhead irrigation
prevention: Rotate crops and destroy infected residues
leaf_blight.yield_risk: 20-60% yield loss if ear leaves affected

downy_mildew.description: Leaf shows yellow streaking and unhealthy pale areas, with weak growth.
stages: Early: Light yellow streaks and fuzzy growth below
Moderate: Pronounced yellow and white streaks become visible
Severe: Severe yellowing, stunting, and leaf death
downy_mildew.cause: Pathogen pressure in wet, cool-to-warm conditions
downy_mildew.favorable_conditions: Wet, humid conditions and infected seed
management: Rogue highly infected plants
Use suitable disease management treatment quickly
Keep field drains clear to reduce moisture buildup
prevention: Use resistant seed and treated seed sources
downy_mildew.yield_risk: 30-70% with early infection, 10-20% with late infection

maize_streak_virus.description: Leaf shows streak-like yellow lines and reduced leaf vigor.
stages: Early: Thin yellow streaks appear on emerging leaves
Moderate: Pronounced yellow streaks and stunting
Severe: Necrotic streaks, severe dwarfing, and poor ears
maize_streak_virus.cause: Viral infection spread by leafhopper insects
maize_streak_virus.favorable_conditions: High vector pressure in nearby fields
management: Remove heavily infected plants early
Control leafhopper vectors quickly
Replant with resistant varieties if outbreak is severe
prevention: Early vector management and field hygiene

maize_lethal_necrosis.description: Leaf shows severe drying/necrosis patterns and strong plant stress.
stages: Early: Chlorotic streaks and initial necrosis
Moderate: Rapid progression with extensive necrosis
Critical: Complete leaf death, plant drying, and plant death
maize_lethal_necrosis.favorable_conditions: Insect vectors and infected crop material
maize_lethal_necrosis.progression_time: 3-5 days from Stage 1 to Stage 2, then 2-3 days to death
management: Remove and isolate heavily infected plants immediately
Control insect vectors urgently
Consult local extension officer without delay
prevention: Plant resistant varieties and use clean seed
Control vectors and maintain strict field sanitation
